content.problemStatement:
The Professor is facing the North. Tokyo is in trouble, and she is facing the South. Professor being her guardian angel wants to help her.

So, The Professor will follow some instructions, given as a string SSS of length NNN, and will turn either left or right according to these instructions. He can save Tokyo only if after following a substring of instructions he will face in the same direction that Tokyo is facing.

Will the Professor be able to save Tokyo?


Input Format

The first line contains an integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The TTT test cases then follow.
The first line of each test case contains NNN.
The second line contains a string that contains only `'L'` and `'R'`, where `'L'` represents left and `'R'` represents right.


Output Format
For each test case, output `"YES"` if the Professor will be able to save Tokyo and `"NO"` otherwise.


Output is case insensitive, which means that "yes", "Yes", "YEs", "no", "nO" - all such strings will be acceptable.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the instruction string. The second line contains a string S of length N consisting only of characters 'L' and 'R'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if there exists a non‑empty contiguous substring of S that makes the Professor face South after starting from North, otherwise output "NO". The answer is case‑insensitive.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: Substring "RLRLLL" gives net rotation +2 (mod 4) → South. Test case 2: No substring yields rotation 2 mod 4. Test case 3: Substring "RR" (or "RL") yields net rotation -2 ≡ 2 mod 4.

solution.approach: Treat a left turn as +1 and a right turn as -1 (each representing a 90° rotation). Starting direction is North (value 0). After executing a substring, the net rotation is the sum of its values. The Professor faces South iff the net rotation is 180°, i.e., the sum ≡ 2 (mod 4). Let pref[i] be the prefix sum modulo 4 up to position i (pref[0]=0). A substring (l+1 … r) has sum ≡ 2 iff pref[r] ≡ pref[l] + 2 (mod 4). While scanning the string we keep a boolean array seen[4] of residues that have appeared as a prefix. For each new prefix p we check if seen[(p + 2) % 4] (or equivalently seen[(p - 2 + 4) % 4]) is true; if so, a required substring exists. After the check we mark seen[p]=true. If the scan finishes without a match, answer is NO. This runs in O(N) time per test case with O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the string. Overall O(total N) ≤ 10^6.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a small array of size 4 is used.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        s = next(it).strip()
        seen = [False]*4
        seen[0] = True
        pref = 0
        ok = False
        for ch in s:
            pref += 1 if ch == 'L' else -1
            pref %= 4
            need = (pref + 2) % 4
            if seen[need]:
                ok = True
                break
            seen[pref] = True
        out_lines.append('YES' if ok else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
